topic.title: MLE vs MAP
topic.question: How do maximum likelihood estimation and maximum a posteriori estimation differ, and when does each give the better answer?

promptVariant.name: Whiteboard prompt v2 - payoff numbers + sync enforcement

lesson.title: MLE vs MAP: When Data Alone Is Not Enough
lesson.learnerLevel: statistics or ML students who know Bayes' rule
lesson.payoff: A Beta(2,2) prior on a coin's bias corrects MLE's embarrassing p-hat=1 estimate after just 3 heads in 3 flips -- one prior choice fixes a failure mode that would require dozens of extra coin flips to self-correct under MLE alone.
staircase: MLE failure mode with tiny data
MLE as the argmax of the likelihood
Bayes' rule connects likelihood to posterior
MAP adds log-prior as a regularizer
MAP shrinks estimates toward the prior
Choosing MLE vs MAP by data quantity and prior quality
lesson.destination: The learner holds theta_MAP = argmax_theta [log P(D|theta) + log P(theta)], recognizes that MLE is the special case with a flat (uniform) prior, and can state when each estimator wins: MLE when data is abundant and a prior would bias the answer, MAP when data is scarce and a calibrated prior prevents overfitting.

narration.fullText: Imagine flipping a coin three times and getting heads every time. Maximum likelihood estimation -- MLE, the parameter value that makes the observed data most probable -- would conclude the coin has a 100% chance of landing heads. That is almost certainly wrong, and today we will see exactly why, and how MAP fixes it. On the board I will draw our coin-flip scenario and mark where MLE breaks. MLE says: find the value of theta, our unknown parameter, that maximizes P of D given theta -- the likelihood, meaning the probability of seeing the data we actually saw, treated as a function of theta rather than of the data. We write theta-hat MLE equals argmax over theta of P of D given theta -- where argmax means the input value that achieves the largest output, not the largest output itself. For three heads, that curve peaks at theta equals 1, giving the absurd estimate. Now let's bring in a prior. Bayes' rule tells us that the posterior -- P of theta given D -- is proportional to the likelihood times the prior P of theta. The prior encodes what we believed about theta before seeing any data. MAP, or maximum a posteriori estimation, finds the theta that maximizes this posterior. Taking logarithms -- which preserve the location of the maximum -- gives us theta-hat MAP equals argmax over theta of the quantity log P of D given theta plus log P of theta. Compare that to MLE: MLE maximizes log P of D given theta alone. MAP adds log P of theta, a regularization term that pulls the estimate toward regions of high prior probability. For our coin, a Beta distribution with parameters alpha equals 2 and beta equals 2 -- a symmetric hump centered at 0.5 -- serves as our prior, encoding the belief that the coin is probably fair. The MAP estimate then balances the three observed heads against that prior belief, landing near 0.6 rather than 1.0. So when does each estimator win? Use MLE when you have abundant data -- the likelihood will dominate and a prior would only bias you. Use MAP when data is scarce or noisy and you have a calibrated prior, because the prior acts as a guard rail against overfitting. The unifying formula to carry with you is theta-hat MAP equals argmax of log-likelihood plus log-prior -- and MLE is simply the special case where log-prior is constant, that is, where you have a uniform prior over all values of theta.

beats.text: Imagine flipping a coin three times and getting heads every time. MLE -- maximum likelihood estimation, the parameter value that makes the observed data most probable -- would conclude the coin has a 100% chance of landing heads. That is almost certainly wrong, and today we will see exactly why, and how MAP fixes it.
beats.whyThisBeat: MLE failure mode with tiny data -- motivates the entire lesson

beats.text: MLE says: find the value of theta, our unknown parameter, that maximizes P of D given theta -- the likelihood, meaning the probability of seeing the data we actually saw, treated as a function of theta rather than of the data. We write theta-hat MLE equals argmax over theta of P of D given theta -- where argmax means the input value that achieves the largest output, not the largest output itself. For three heads, that likelihood curve peaks at theta equals 1.
beats.whyThisBeat: MLE as the argmax of the likelihood -- installs the formal definition

beats.text: Now let's bring in a prior. Bayes' rule tells us that the posterior -- P of theta given D -- is proportional to the likelihood times the prior P of theta. The prior encodes what we believed about theta before seeing any data.
beats.whyThisBeat: Bayes' rule connects likelihood to posterior -- introduces the prior

beats.text: MAP, or maximum a posteriori estimation, finds the theta that maximizes this posterior. Taking logarithms -- which preserve the location of the maximum -- gives us theta-hat MAP equals argmax over theta of log P of D given theta plus log P of theta. Compare that to MLE: MLE maximizes log P of D given theta alone. MAP adds log P of theta, a regularization term that pulls the estimate toward regions of high prior probability.
beats.whyThisBeat: MAP adds log-prior as a regularizer -- derives the key formula

beats.text: For our coin, a Beta distribution with parameters alpha equals 2 and beta equals 2 -- a symmetric hump centered at 0.5 -- serves as our prior. The MAP estimate balances the three observed heads against that prior belief, landing near 0.6 rather than 1.0. That single prior choice corrects the failure mode without any new data.
beats.whyThisBeat: MAP shrinks estimates toward the prior -- makes regularization concrete

beats.text: So when does each estimator win? Use MLE when you have abundant data -- the likelihood dominates and a prior would only bias you. Use MAP when data is scarce or noisy and you have a calibrated prior, because the prior acts as a guard rail against overfitting to a small sample.
beats.whyThisBeat: Choosing MLE vs MAP by data quantity and prior quality -- actionable decision rule

beats.text: The formula to carry with you is theta-hat MAP equals argmax of log-likelihood plus log-prior. MLE is simply the special case where log-prior is constant -- a uniform prior over all values of theta. That single equation unifies both estimators.
beats.whyThisBeat: Synthesis -- installs the unifying formula and the MLE-as-special-case insight

actions.content: MLE vs MAP
Three coin icons arranged left to right labeled H, H, H, with a question mark above a theta symbol beneath them, and an arrow pointing right to the text p-hat MLE = 1.0 circled in red
circle the p-hat MLE = 1.0 label in red to flag it as the failure case
theta-hat_MLE = argmax_{theta} P(D | theta)

A smooth curve on labeled axes -- horizontal axis labeled theta from 0 to 1, vertical axis labeled P(D|theta) -- rising monotonically and peaking sharply at theta=1, with a dot at the peak labeled theta-hat MLE = 1
underline argmax in blue to emphasize it means the input achieving the maximum, not the maximum value itself
draw a vertical dashed red line at theta=1 and label it MLE collapses here to show the degenerate peak
P(theta | D) proportional to P(D | theta) * P(theta)
P(theta) = prior: belief about theta before data
box P(theta) in green to mark it as the new ingredient MAP adds over MLE
theta-hat_MAP = argmax_{theta} [ log P(D|theta) + log P(theta) ]
underline log P(theta) in green and annotate regularizer -- pulls theta toward prior

Two curves on shared axes -- horizontal axis labeled theta from 0 to 1, one red curve labeled log-likelihood peaking at theta=1, one green curve labeled log-prior Beta(2,2) peaking at theta=0.5 as a smooth hump -- their sum shown as a dashed blue curve peaking near theta=0.6, with a vertical dashed line at 0.6 labeled theta-hat MAP
Prior: Beta(alpha=2, beta=2) -- symmetric hump centered at 0.5, encodes coin is probably fair
draw a bracket between theta=0.6 and theta=1.0 on the horizontal axis and label it shrinkage toward prior
Use MLE: large data, no reliable prior  |  Use MAP: small data, calibrated prior
box small data, calibrated prior in green to flag the MAP use case
all
theta-hat_MAP = argmax [ log P(D|theta) + log P(theta) ]   MLE: log P(theta) = const (uniform prior)
box the entire equation in blue and annotate MLE is MAP with flat prior to seal the unifying insight
all
